segments.title: Hiroshima as a thriving city beside its memorials
segments.summary: A view from the memorial area leads to a reflection on Hiroshima as a living, rebuilt city rather than a place frozen by the bombing.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes arriving in Hiroshima expecting its past to dominate the city, then finding a busy and attractive modern community. From the memorial area near the dome, he looks across the place where the bomb cleared a vast space and contrasts that destruction with the city now around it. The moment treats commemoration and recovery as neighbours rather than opposites. It is a useful watch point for thinking about how a city can preserve evidence of catastrophe without allowing that event to become its only identity. His recommendation of the memorial route is personal travel advice, not a historical survey, but it frames the later museum reflection.

evidence.note: He contrasts an expectation of a city defined by its past with the thriving city seen from the memorial area and calls the museum devastatingly effective.

segments.title: Museum objects and the human cost of the Hiroshima bombing
segments.summary: The Hiroshima museum makes familiar facts about the bombing immediate through stories of illness, bereavement, and a child's reconstructed school uniform.
segments.body: Although he says that naval and maritime warfare are his specialist fields, Dr. Clarke distinguishes knowing the facts and figures from encountering the human consequences in a museum. A story about a girl who developed cancer after the bomb, yet tried to sustain happiness around her, gives that loss a personal scale. He is affected even more strongly by a small school uniform assembled from the remaining pieces of three children who had died. Relating the uniform to his own primary-school memories lets him imagine ordinary friends in the victims' place. This is a reflective account rather than a detailed explanation of the bombing, but it shows why material objects can make civilian suffering harder to treat as an abstraction.

evidence.note: He contrasts facts and figures with museum stories of cancer after the bomb and a school uniform made from three dead children's remains.
